_meta.authoringNotes: Classification and primary-vs-secondary distinction per Wigley FM, Flavahan NA. NEJM 2016 (PMID 27410428). Epidemiology per Garner R et al. BMJ Open 2015 and population studies. Treatment evidence per Rirash F et al. Cochrane 2017 on calcium channel blockers (PMID 29237099) and the BSR/EULAR systemic sclerosis management guidance (Kowal-Bielecka O et al. Ann Rheum Dis 2017, PMID 27941129). Nailfold capillaroscopy and secondary Raynaud's prediction per Koenig M et al. Arthritis Rheum 2008. Digital ulcer management and prostacyclin/endothelin antagonist data per systemic sclerosis literature. Connective tissue disease screening per ACR/EULAR criteria.

heroOverview: Raynaud's phenomenon is an exaggerated vasospastic response of the small arteries supplying the fingers and toes — and sometimes the ears, nose, and nipples — to cold exposure or emotional stress. During an attack the affected digits classically turn white (ischemia), then blue (deoxygenation), then red (reperfusion), accompanied by numbness, tingling, and pain. It affects an estimated 3-5% of the general population, with women affected three to four times more often than men and onset of the primary form usually before age 30. The crucial clinical task is separating primary Raynaud's — a benign, idiopathic condition with normal blood vessels and no underlying disease — from secondary Raynaud's, which is caused by an identifiable disorder, most importantly systemic sclerosis and other connective tissue diseases, and which can lead to digital ulcers, scarring, and tissue loss. Nailfold capillaroscopy and autoantibody testing are central to making that distinction. Most people with primary Raynaud's are managed with cold avoidance and lifestyle measures alone; calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine are first-line drug therapy, and secondary Raynaud's with digital ulcers may need prostacyclin analogues, phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, or endothelin receptor antagonists.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 3-5% of the general population; primary Raynaud's accounts for around 80-90% of cases
keyStats.demographics: Women affected 3-4 times more often than men; primary form onset usually before age 30, secondary form onset typically after age 30
keyStats.avgAge: Primary Raynaud's typically begins in the teens to twenties; new-onset Raynaud's after age 40 raises suspicion of a secondary cause
keyStats.globalCases: Tens of millions affected worldwide; more common in colder climates

definition: Raynaud's phenomenon (ICD-10: I73.00 without gangrene, I73.01 with gangrene) is episodic, reversible vasospasm of the digital arteries, precapillary arterioles, and cutaneous arteriovenous shunts, triggered by cold or emotional stress and producing a characteristic sequence of color change in the fingers and toes. It is divided into two forms with very different implications. Primary Raynaud's phenomenon (formerly Raynaud's disease) is idiopathic, has no underlying associated disease, occurs in structurally normal vessels, and follows a benign course; it is defined by symmetrical attacks, absence of tissue necrosis or gangrene, a normal nailfold capillary pattern, negative or low-titre antinuclear antibody, and normal inflammatory markers. Secondary Raynaud's phenomenon is caused by an identifiable disorder — most importantly the connective tissue diseases (systemic sclerosis, mixed connective tissue disease, systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren syndrome, dermatomyositis), but also occlusive arterial disease, occupational vibration injury, certain drugs, and haematological conditions. In secondary Raynaud's the underlying disease produces structural vascular abnormality, so attacks can be more severe, asymmetrical, and complicated by digital ulcers, pitting scars, and critical ischemia. The pathophysiology involves an imbalance between vasoconstrictor mechanisms (enhanced alpha-2c adrenoceptor activity, endothelin-1, reduced nitric oxide) and vasodilator mechanisms, with structural vessel disease compounding this in the secondary form. Raynaud's is managed by primary care for uncomplicated primary disease and by rheumatology, with vascular medicine and dermatology input, when a secondary cause or complications are present.

typesClassification.description: Idiopathic, benign vasospasm with no underlying disease, normal nailfold capillaries, negative or low-titre ANA, and normal inflammatory markers. Accounts for 80-90% of cases. Attacks are symmetrical and do not cause tissue loss.

Caused by an identifiable disorder, most often a connective tissue disease. Features that suggest a secondary cause include onset after age 30, asymmetrical or severe attacks, digital ulcers or pitting scars, abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy, and positive autoantibodies.

Seen in systemic sclerosis (where Raynaud's is nearly universal and often the first symptom), mixed connective tissue disease, lupus, Sjögren syndrome, and dermatomyositis. Carries the highest risk of digital ulcers and ischemic complications.

typesClassification.type: Occupational and vibration-induced Raynaud's (hand-arm vibration syndrome)
typesClassification.description: Caused by long-term use of vibrating tools (grinders, chainsaws, jackhammers). Often asymmetrical, affecting the dominant or more-exposed hand, and is a recognized occupational disease.

Triggered by drugs (beta-blockers, certain chemotherapy agents, ergot derivatives, stimulants, sympathomimetics), occlusive arterial disease, hyperviscosity and cryoglobulinemia, and thoracic outlet syndrome.

primarySymptoms: Episodic, sharply demarcated color change in the fingers or toes triggered by cold or emotional stress — classically white (pallor) then blue (cyanosis) then red (reperfusion hyperemia), though not all three phases occur in every person.
Numbness, tingling, and a cold sensation in the affected digits during an attack.
Throbbing pain and a burning sensation as blood flow returns and the digits turn red.
Attacks lasting from a few minutes to over an hour, resolving with rewarming.
Symmetrical involvement of multiple fingers of both hands in primary Raynaud's; the thumbs are often spared.
Involvement of the toes, and sometimes the ears, nose, lips, or nipples.
In secondary Raynaud's, more severe and prolonged attacks, sometimes asymmetrical, with persistent pain.
Digital pitting scars, ulcers at the fingertips, or areas of dead tissue (gangrene) in severe secondary Raynaud's.
Skin tightening, puffy fingers, telangiectasia, or calcinosis in patients whose Raynaud's is part of systemic sclerosis.
Associated dry eyes and mouth, joint pain, rash, or muscle weakness when Raynaud's is part of another connective tissue disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Fingers that consistently turn white or blue and feel numb when exposed to cold or stress
New-onset Raynaud's after age 40, which raises the possibility of a secondary cause
Asymmetrical attacks affecting one hand more than the other
Attacks accompanied by puffy fingers, skin tightening, or fingertip sores
Raynaud's appearing alongside joint pain, rashes, dry eyes and mouth, or muscle weakness
emergencySigns: A persistently white, blue, or dusky digit that does not rewarm — critical digital ischemia requiring urgent vascular and rheumatology assessment
A new fingertip ulcer or area of blackened, dead tissue (digital gangrene)
Severe unrelenting digital pain with loss of sensation
Sudden severe Raynaud's affecting a single digit, especially in an older patient — exclude embolism or arterial occlusion
Rapidly progressive skin tightening, breathlessness, or swallowing difficulty in a patient with Raynaud's — possible aggressive systemic sclerosis

causes.description: In primary Raynaud's there is no underlying disease. An exaggerated, reversible vasoconstrictor response to cold and stress arises from increased activity of alpha-2c adrenoceptors on digital vessels and an imbalance of vasoconstrictor and vasodilator signaling, in structurally normal arteries.

causes.cause: Systemic sclerosis and other connective tissue diseases
causes.description: Systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) is the most important secondary cause — Raynaud's is present in over 95% of patients and is often the first symptom. Lupus, mixed connective tissue disease, Sjögren syndrome, and dermatomyositis also cause secondary Raynaud's, with structural vascular damage that makes attacks more severe.
Long-term use of vibrating power tools damages digital vessels and nerves, producing vibration-induced white finger. It is often asymmetrical and is a recognized, compensable occupational disease.

Beta-blockers, ergot derivatives, certain chemotherapy agents (notably bleomycin and cisplatin), interferons, sympathomimetic decongestants, and stimulant medications can precipitate or worsen Raynaud's.

Atherosclerosis, thromboangiitis obliterans (Buerger disease), thoracic outlet syndrome, and embolic disease reduce digital blood flow and can present with secondary Raynaud's, often asymmetrical and in older patients.
Cryoglobulinemia, cold agglutinin disease, polycythemia, and other hyperviscosity states impair digital perfusion and can cause secondary Raynaud's.

riskFactors.description: Women are affected 3-4 times more often than men, particularly with primary Raynaud's.

riskFactors.factor: Young age at onset (primary) or age over 40 (secondary)
riskFactors.description: Primary Raynaud's usually starts in the teens and twenties; new onset after age 40 raises concern for a secondary cause.
Primary Raynaud's shows familial clustering, and a first-degree relative with Raynaud's increases risk.
Systemic sclerosis, lupus, mixed connective tissue disease, Sjögren syndrome, and dermatomyositis are the most important causes of secondary Raynaud's.
Long-term use of vibrating tools causes hand-arm vibration syndrome; reducing exposure and using anti-vibration equipment lowers risk.
Smoking causes vasoconstriction and accelerates vascular disease; it worsens attacks and increases the risk of digital ulcers, especially in secondary Raynaud's.

riskFactors.factor: Cold climate and cold occupational exposure
riskFactors.description: Raynaud's is more common and more troublesome in colder climates and in workers with frequent cold exposure.
Beta-blockers, ergot derivatives, some chemotherapy agents, and sympathomimetics can precipitate or worsen Raynaud's; reviewing and adjusting these helps.

affectedDemographics: Raynaud's phenomenon affects an estimated 3-5% of the general population, with higher rates in colder climates.
Women are affected 3-4 times more often than men.
Primary Raynaud's typically begins in adolescence or early adulthood and is most common in young women.
Secondary Raynaud's tends to begin later, often after age 30-40, and carries the demographics of the underlying disease.
Raynaud's is present in over 95% of people with systemic sclerosis and frequently precedes other features of that disease by years.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of Raynaud's phenomenon is clinical, based on a characteristic history of episodic, cold- or stress-induced, sharply demarcated color change in the digits with numbness and pain. The central diagnostic task is then to distinguish primary from secondary Raynaud's, because this determines prognosis and the intensity of management. Features pointing to primary Raynaud's include onset before age 30, symmetrical attacks, no tissue damage, no symptoms of an underlying disease, normal nailfold capillaroscopy, negative or low-titre antinuclear antibody, and normal inflammatory markers. Features pointing to a secondary cause include onset after age 30, asymmetrical or severe attacks, digital pitting scars or ulcers, and any symptoms or signs of a connective tissue disease. Nailfold capillaroscopy — examining the capillary loops at the base of the fingernail under magnification — is the single most useful test: a normal pattern strongly supports primary Raynaud's, while enlarged, dilated, or absent capillary loops (the 'scleroderma pattern') strongly predicts current or future systemic sclerosis. Blood tests include antinuclear antibody (and, if positive, extractable nuclear antigen antibodies including anti-centromere and anti-Scl-70), inflammatory markers, and a full blood count. Hand-arm vibration syndrome is diagnosed from occupational history. In older patients with asymmetrical disease, arterial imaging may be needed to exclude occlusive disease or embolism. Patients who initially appear to have primary Raynaud's but have an abnormal capillaroscopy or positive antibodies require ongoing monitoring, as a proportion will declare an underlying connective tissue disease over the following years.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the characteristic episodic color change and screens for features of an underlying connective tissue disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A consultation reviewing attack pattern, triggers, occupational and drug history, and a focused examination of the hands, skin, and joints. Photographs of attacks taken by the patient are very helpful.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes primary from secondary Raynaud's and predicts systemic sclerosis — the single most useful test
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless examination of the nailfold capillaries under magnification (dermoscope or videocapillaroscope), taking 10-20 minutes. A normal pattern supports primary disease; a 'scleroderma pattern' indicates a secondary cause.

diagnosticTests.test: Antinuclear antibody (ANA) and connective tissue disease antibodies
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for connective tissue disease; specific antibodies (anti-centromere, anti-Scl-70/topoisomerase, anti-RNP) point to particular diagnoses
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within several days to two weeks. A strongly positive ANA in a patient with Raynaud's raises suspicion of a secondary cause.

diagnosticTests.test: Inflammatory markers and full blood count
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects inflammation, anemia, and haematological causes of secondary Raynaud's
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 24-48 hours. These are typically normal in primary Raynaud's.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used selectively in older patients or those with asymmetrical or severe disease to exclude occlusive arterial disease, embolism, or thoracic outlet syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive Doppler ultrasound, or CT/catheter angiography when a fixed arterial lesion is suspected.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hand-arm vibration syndrome and drug-induced Raynaud's
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured review of vibrating-tool exposure and current medications during the consultation.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of Raynaud's phenomenon is matched to its severity and to whether it is primary or secondary. For primary Raynaud's and mild secondary Raynaud's, non-drug measures are usually sufficient: keeping the whole body and the extremities warm, wearing gloves and warm layered clothing, using hand and foot warmers, avoiding sudden cold exposure, stopping smoking, managing stress, and reviewing and where possible stopping medications that provoke vasospasm (such as beta-blockers and stimulant decongestants). When attacks are frequent or interfere with daily life, first-line drug treatment is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker — most commonly nifedipine (slow-release), or amlodipine — which reduces the frequency and severity of attacks. Side effects include headache, flushing, ankle swelling, and low blood pressure, and the dose is titrated to balance benefit and tolerability. Second-line oral options include phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil), topical nitrates (glyceryl trinitrate ointment applied to the affected digits), angiotensin receptor blockers, and selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors such as fluoxetine. Secondary Raynaud's, particularly in systemic sclerosis, requires more aggressive treatment and management of the underlying disease. For severe attacks, recurrent or non-healing digital ulcers, or critical digital ischemia, intravenous prostacyclin analogues (iloprost) are used in hospital, and the endothelin receptor antagonist bosentan reduces the development of new digital ulcers in systemic sclerosis. Digital sympathectomy and, in critical ischemia, intra-arterial vasodilators or botulinum toxin injection are options at specialist centres. Aspirin or other antiplatelet therapy is considered in secondary Raynaud's with ischemic complications. Wound care, infection treatment, and analgesia are essential for digital ulcers. Throughout, patient education on cold avoidance, prompt management of attacks, and recognizing warning signs of critical ischemia is central to good outcomes.

medicalTreatments.name: Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers (nifedipine slow-release 10-60 mg/day, amlodipine 5-10 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line drug treatment for Raynaud's. Reduces the frequency and severity of attacks by promoting digital vasodilation. Side effects include headache, flushing, ankle edema, and hypotension; the dose is titrated to tolerability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces attack frequency by roughly one-third and lessens attack severity (Rirash Cochrane 2017, PMID 29237099)

medicalTreatments.name: Phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors (sildenafil, tadalafil)
medicalTreatments.description: Second-line oral vasodilators that improve digital blood flow and can aid digital ulcer healing in secondary Raynaud's. Side effects include headache, flushing, and nasal congestion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduce attack frequency and severity and support digital ulcer healing in systemic sclerosis-associated Raynaud's

medicalTreatments.name: Topical nitrates (glyceryl trinitrate ointment)
medicalTreatments.description: Applied to the base of affected digits to produce local vasodilation. Useful as add-on therapy; the main side effect is headache.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in attack frequency and severity; useful adjunct to oral therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous prostacyclin analogues (iloprost)
medicalTreatments.description: Given as an inpatient infusion over several days for severe attacks, critical digital ischemia, and digital ulcers, particularly in systemic sclerosis. Side effects include headache, flushing, jaw pain, and hypotension.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces severity of attacks and aids healing of digital ulcers; benefit can persist for weeks after a course

medicalTreatments.name: Endothelin receptor antagonist (bosentan)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral agent used in systemic sclerosis to reduce the development of new digital ulcers. Requires monitoring of liver function and is teratogenic, so reliable contraception is needed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces the number of new digital ulcers in systemic sclerosis in randomized trials; does not heal existing ulcers

medicalTreatments.name: Other oral vasodilators (angiotensin receptor blockers, fluoxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: Losartan and the SSRI fluoxetine have evidence as alternative or add-on therapy when calcium channel blockers are not tolerated or insufficient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Modest reduction in attack frequency and severity; useful when first-line agents are poorly tolerated

medicalTreatments.name: Antiplatelet therapy and wound care for digital ulcers
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose aspirin is considered in secondary Raynaud's with ischemic complications; digital ulcers need meticulous wound care, infection treatment, and analgesia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supports digital ulcer healing and reduces complications when combined with vasodilator therapy

surgicalOptions.name: Digital sympathectomy
surgicalOptions.description: A microsurgical procedure stripping the sympathetic nerve fibres around the digital arteries to reduce vasospasm. Reserved for severe, refractory secondary Raynaud's with recurrent ulcers or critical ischemia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improves ulcer healing and reduces pain in selected refractory patients; benefit may diminish over time

surgicalOptions.name: Botulinum toxin injection
surgicalOptions.description: Injection of botulinum toxin around the digital neurovascular bundles to reduce vasospasm. Used at specialist centres for refractory disease and critical ischemia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Improvement in pain and perfusion in case series; evidence base still developing

surgicalOptions.name: Debridement or amputation for digital gangrene
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical debridement of necrotic tissue, or amputation of an irreversibly gangrenous digit, in advanced critical ischemia. A last resort after vasodilator therapy has failed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Removes non-viable tissue and controls infection; the aim is to preserve as much functional digit as possible

alternativeTreatments.name: Hand and foot warmers and thermal biofeedback
alternativeTreatments.description: Battery-operated or chemical warmers prevent and abort attacks; thermal biofeedback training, in which patients learn to raise their finger temperature, has modest supporting evidence and no side effects.

alternativeTreatments.name: Behavioural cold-avoidance and stress-management programs
alternativeTreatments.description: Structured education on whole-body warming, layered clothing, prompt rewarming, and stress reduction reduces attack frequency and is the foundation of self-management.

whySeeSpecialist: Uncomplicated primary Raynaud's can be managed in primary care, but referral to rheumatology is warranted when there are features of a secondary cause — onset after age 30, asymmetrical or severe attacks, digital ulcers or pitting scars, abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy, or positive autoantibodies. Rheumatology can confirm or exclude systemic sclerosis and other connective tissue diseases, arrange capillaroscopy, and coordinate advanced treatment of digital ulcers and critical ischemia.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatology service with access to nailfold capillaroscopy and experience in connective tissue disease, ideally a scleroderma or vasculopathy clinic for patients with digital ulcers or suspected systemic sclerosis. Vascular medicine input is valuable when occlusive arterial disease is suspected, particularly in older patients with asymmetrical disease.
hospitalCriteria: Access to connective tissue disease autoantibody testing
Capacity for inpatient intravenous iloprost infusion
Vascular medicine and imaging for suspected occlusive arterial disease
Specialist wound care and digital ulcer management
keyFacilities: Occupational medicine services for hand-arm vibration syndrome
preventionStrategies: Keep the whole body warm, not just the hands — wear layered clothing, hats, and warm footwear, since core cooling triggers digital vasospasm.
Wear gloves or mittens before going into the cold, and use battery-operated or chemical hand and foot warmers.
Avoid sudden changes in temperature, such as reaching into a freezer or moving quickly into air conditioning.
Stop smoking, as nicotine causes vasoconstriction and worsens attacks and ulcer risk.
Review medications with a doctor and, where possible, avoid beta-blockers, ergot derivatives, and stimulant decongestants that provoke vasospasm.
Use anti-vibration tools and gloves and limit exposure time for those who work with vibrating equipment.
Manage stress, since emotional stress is a common attack trigger.
lifestyleModifications: Dress in warm layers and keep the core, hands, and feet warm at all times in cold weather.
Run warm (not hot) water over the hands or place them in warm water to abort an attack early.
Gently rotate the arms or wiggle the fingers to encourage blood flow during an attack.
Stop smoking and avoid second-hand smoke.
Limit caffeine if it appears to trigger attacks.
Practice relaxation and stress-reduction techniques to reduce stress-triggered attacks.
Inspect the fingertips regularly for sores or ulcers and seek prompt care if any appear.

recommended: Warm meals and hot drinks, particularly in cold weather
A balanced diet supporting cardiovascular health (vegetables, fruit, fish, whole grains)
Adequate iron intake if anemia is present
Foods rich in omega-3 fatty acids, which have modest supporting evidence for vascular health
avoid: Excess caffeine if it appears to trigger attacks
Excess alcohol, which can cause flushing followed by cooling of the extremities
Crash diets that leave the body underfuelled and prone to feeling cold

exerciseGuidelines: Regular physical activity improves circulation and cardiovascular health and is encouraged. Warm up indoors before exercising in cold weather and wear warm gloves and layers for outdoor activity. Arm-swinging and finger movements can be used to abort an early attack. Avoid vibrating gym or work equipment if it provokes symptoms.
dailyManagement: Carry gloves and hand warmers and put them on before cold exposure.
Keep the core body warm with layered clothing throughout the day.
Abort early attacks promptly with warm water and gentle movement.
Take prescribed vasodilator medication consistently as directed.
Check fingertips and toes daily for new sores, especially in secondary Raynaud's.
Avoid known triggers — sudden cold, stress, and vasoconstricting drugs — wherever possible.
Seek prompt medical review for a digit that will not rewarm or for any new ulcer.
prognosis: The prognosis of Raynaud's phenomenon depends almost entirely on whether it is primary or secondary. Primary Raynaud's has an excellent prognosis: it is benign, does not cause tissue damage, often improves over time, and in a proportion of people resolves spontaneously, particularly in those who move to warmer climates or whose symptoms began in adolescence. It does not progress to a connective tissue disease in the great majority of cases. Secondary Raynaud's carries the prognosis of its underlying cause. In systemic sclerosis it can be severe and progressive, with a substantial lifetime risk of digital ulcers, pitting scars, and, in a minority, critical ischemia and tissue loss; aggressive vasodilator therapy and treatment of the underlying disease improve these outcomes. Patients who present with apparently primary Raynaud's but have an abnormal nailfold capillaroscopy or positive autoantibodies are at increased risk of developing a connective tissue disease over the following years, and around 12-15% in some series transition to a definite secondary diagnosis; these patients warrant ongoing monitoring. Overall, early identification of the secondary forms, prompt treatment of digital ulcers, smoking cessation, and good cold-avoidance practice substantially reduce complications.
recoveryTimeline: Individual attacks last minutes to over an hour and resolve fully with rewarming. Lifestyle measures reduce attack frequency within weeks. Calcium channel blockers show benefit within days to weeks of reaching an effective dose. Digital ulcers in secondary Raynaud's take weeks to months to heal with vasodilator therapy and wound care. Primary Raynaud's often improves gradually over years and may resolve spontaneously.
complications: Digital ulcers at the fingertips, which are painful, slow to heal, and prone to infection — mainly in secondary Raynaud's.
Digital pitting scars from previous ischemic damage.
Critical digital ischemia and gangrene with risk of tissue loss or amputation in severe secondary disease.
Secondary infection of digital ulcers, including osteomyelitis.
Chronic pain and reduced hand function affecting work and daily activities.
Progression to or unmasking of an underlying connective tissue disease, particularly systemic sclerosis.
Side effects of vasodilator drugs, including headache, flushing, hypotension, and ankle swelling.

supportResources.name: Scleroderma & Raynaud's UK (SRUK)
supportResources.description: UK charity providing information, support, and research funding for people with Raynaud's and scleroderma.

supportResources.name: Raynaud's Association (US)
supportResources.description: US patient organisation offering education, cold-weather resources, and peer support.

supportResources.name: Scleroderma Foundation
supportResources.description: US organisation supporting people with systemic sclerosis, the most important secondary cause of Raynaud's.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology
supportResources.description: Professional body with patient-facing information on Raynaud's phenomenon and connective tissue diseases.

confusedWithConditions.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Systemic sclerosis is the most important disease behind secondary Raynaud's, not a separate competing diagnosis — Raynaud's is present in over 95% of cases. The distinction that matters is between primary Raynaud's (benign, no underlying disease) and Raynaud's that is the first sign of evolving systemic sclerosis, identified by abnormal capillaroscopy and autoantibodies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lupus is another connective tissue disease that causes secondary Raynaud's. It is distinguished by additional features such as malar rash, photosensitivity, serositis, kidney involvement, and a positive ANA with lupus-specific antibodies, alongside the Raynaud's.

confusedWithConditions.name: Peripheral Artery Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peripheral artery disease causes fixed reduction in blood flow from atherosclerotic narrowing, typically in older patients with risk factors, producing persistent rather than episodic, reversible color change. Raynaud's is episodic vasospasm with full recovery between attacks.

confusedWithConditions.name: Complex regional pain syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CRPS follows a limb injury and features persistent regional pain with autonomic, trophic, and motor changes affecting a whole region, not the episodic, cold-triggered, sharply demarcated digital color change of Raynaud's.

relatedConditions.name: Scleroderma (Systemic Sclerosis)
relatedConditions.relevance: The most important secondary cause of Raynaud's; Raynaud's is often its first symptom and capillaroscopy predicts it.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus
relatedConditions.relevance: A connective tissue disease that commonly causes secondary Raynaud's.

relatedConditions.name: Antiphospholipid Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: A thrombotic disorder that can coexist with connective tissue disease and contribute to digital ischemia.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequently coexists with primary Raynaud's in young women and shares features of autonomic dysregulation.

relatedConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Another common cause of hand numbness and tingling that may need to be distinguished from or coexist with Raynaud's.

faqs.question: What is Raynaud's phenomenon?
faqs.answer: Raynaud's phenomenon is an exaggerated narrowing of the small arteries in the fingers and toes in response to cold or stress. During an attack the digits classically turn white, then blue, then red, with numbness and pain, before recovering with rewarming.

faqs.question: What is the difference between primary and secondary Raynaud's?
faqs.answer: Primary Raynaud's is idiopathic, benign, occurs in normal blood vessels, and has no underlying disease. Secondary Raynaud's is caused by an identifiable disorder — most often a connective tissue disease such as systemic sclerosis — and can lead to digital ulcers and tissue damage.

faqs.question: What triggers a Raynaud's attack?
faqs.answer: The most common triggers are cold exposure — including air conditioning, freezers, and cold water — and emotional stress. Sudden temperature changes are particularly provocative. Some medications, such as beta-blockers and stimulant decongestants, can also precipitate attacks.

faqs.question: Is Raynaud's phenomenon dangerous?
faqs.answer: Primary Raynaud's is benign and does not cause tissue damage. Secondary Raynaud's can be more serious — severe cases, particularly in systemic sclerosis, can cause painful digital ulcers and, rarely, critical ischemia and tissue loss. The key is identifying which type a person has.

faqs.question: How is Raynaud's phenomenon diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is clinical, based on the characteristic episodic color change. The main task is to separate primary from secondary Raynaud's using nailfold capillaroscopy (examining capillaries at the nail base), antinuclear antibody testing, and inflammatory markers, alongside screening for connective tissue disease symptoms.

faqs.question: What is nailfold capillaroscopy?
faqs.answer: Nailfold capillaroscopy is a painless examination of the tiny capillaries at the base of the fingernail under magnification. A normal pattern supports benign primary Raynaud's, while a 'scleroderma pattern' of enlarged or absent loops strongly predicts systemic sclerosis or another secondary cause.

faqs.question: How is Raynaud's phenomenon treated?
faqs.answer: Most people are managed with cold avoidance, warm clothing, hand warmers, smoking cessation, and stress management. When attacks are frequent, calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine are first-line. Severe secondary Raynaud's may need phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, intravenous iloprost, or endothelin receptor antagonists.

faqs.question: Can Raynaud's phenomenon be cured?
faqs.answer: Primary Raynaud's often improves over time and can resolve spontaneously, especially when it began in adolescence; it does not usually need a cure beyond lifestyle measures. Secondary Raynaud's is managed alongside its underlying cause and is generally a long-term condition.

faqs.question: Does Raynaud's mean I have an autoimmune disease?
faqs.answer: Not usually — around 80-90% of cases are primary Raynaud's with no underlying disease. However, secondary Raynaud's can be the first sign of a connective tissue disease such as systemic sclerosis or lupus, which is why testing to separate the two types is important.

faqs.question: What should I do during a Raynaud's attack?
faqs.answer: Move somewhere warm, run warm (not hot) water over the hands or soak them in warm water, and gently move the fingers or swing the arms to encourage blood flow. Most attacks resolve within minutes to an hour once the body and digits rewarm.

faqs.question: Why does Raynaud's affect women more than men?
faqs.answer: Women are affected 3-4 times more often than men, particularly with primary Raynaud's. The reasons are not fully understood but are thought to involve hormonal influences on blood vessel reactivity and differences in vascular tone regulation.

faqs.question: Can smoking make Raynaud's worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Nicotine causes the blood vessels to constrict, which worsens the frequency and severity of attacks and increases the risk of digital ulcers, especially in secondary Raynaud's. Stopping smoking is one of the most important self-management steps.

faqs.question: What is hand-arm vibration syndrome?
faqs.answer: Hand-arm vibration syndrome is a form of secondary Raynaud's caused by long-term use of vibrating power tools such as grinders, chainsaws, and jackhammers. It is often asymmetrical, affecting the more-exposed hand, and is a recognized occupational disease.

faqs.question: When should I worry about Raynaud's?
faqs.answer: Seek prompt medical attention if a digit stays white, blue, or dusky and will not rewarm, if you develop a fingertip sore or area of dead tissue, or if Raynaud's appears alongside skin tightening, joint pain, rashes, or breathlessness. New-onset Raynaud's after age 40 also warrants assessment.

faqs.question: Can Raynaud's affect parts of the body other than the fingers?
faqs.answer: Yes. Although the fingers are most commonly affected, Raynaud's can also involve the toes and, less often, the ears, nose, lips, and nipples. The same episodic color change and discomfort occur in these areas.

faqs.question: Are calcium channel blockers safe for Raynaud's?
faqs.answer: Calcium channel blockers such as nifedipine are first-line and generally safe, but they can cause headache, facial flushing, ankle swelling, and low blood pressure. The dose is started low and titrated up to balance benefit against side effects; many people tolerate them well.

faqs.question: Will primary Raynaud's turn into scleroderma?
faqs.answer: The great majority of people with primary Raynaud's never develop scleroderma. However, some people who appear to have primary Raynaud's but have abnormal capillaroscopy or positive autoantibodies do go on to develop a connective tissue disease, which is why these patients are monitored over time.

faqs.question: Does Raynaud's get worse in cold weather?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cold exposure is the leading trigger, so attacks are more frequent and more severe in winter and in cold climates and occupations. Many people find their symptoms ease considerably in warmer weather or warmer locations.

faqs.question: Can stress trigger Raynaud's attacks?
faqs.answer: Yes. Emotional stress activates the sympathetic nervous system, which constricts the small digital arteries and can trigger an attack even without cold exposure. Stress-management and relaxation techniques are part of standard self-management.

faqs.question: What happens if a Raynaud's digital ulcer does not heal?
faqs.answer: A non-healing fingertip ulcer needs urgent specialist care. Treatment includes vasodilator drugs (including intravenous iloprost), meticulous wound care, treatment of any infection, and pain control. In severe refractory cases, digital sympathectomy or botulinum toxin injection may be considered, and rarely amputation of gangrenous tissue is needed.

metaDescription: Raynaud's phenomenon: cold-triggered finger color change and pain. Primary vs secondary causes, capillaroscopy, nifedipine and iloprost reviewed.
